MEDIANAMA Impact of ​ SIM Binding on Social Media December 2025 MEDIANAMA www.medianama.com On November 28, India’s Department of Telecommunications (DoT) issued a directive mandating app-based messaging applications, including WhatsApp and Telegram, to implement continuous SIM–device binding, effectively requiring the continuous presence of a SIM for uninterrupted access to these messaging services.

The directive marks a significant shift in how messaging platforms operate and has implications for multi-device access, cloud-based workflows, remote collaboration, and cross-border account use, impacting everyday communication for individuals, creators, and businesses. These new directions, which have been sent to WhatsApp, Telegram, Signal, Arattai, Snapchat, ShareChat, JioChat, and Josh, effectively recognise these companies as Telecommunication Identifier User Entities (TIUEs).

While the measure is positioned as a response to prevent cyber fraud linked to SIM cards, it does not directly address the most prevalent cyber frauds in India, such as phishing, caller ID spoofing, OTP theft, SIM swaps, and VoIP-based impersonation.

Instead, the directive introduces additional friction for legitimate users by disrupting persistent login sessions essential for business messaging and CRM operations, and by complicating access for travellers, NRIs, and users reliant on desktop or web-based clients. Additionally, to ensure persistent SIM binding, the above-mentioned TIUEs are required to ensure that users are logged out periodically, no later than every six hours, and to provide a QR-code-based mechanism for relinking accounts.

Against this backdrop, MediaNama organised a discussion titled “Impact of SIM Binding on Social Media” to examine the policy intent and rationale behind the directive, its technical feasibility, and its broader operational and user-level implications.

Our objective was to identify:​ ●​ The scope of the compliance burden that the draft amendment imposes on businesses ●​ The technical implications of mandatory SIM–device coupling and viable alternatives aligned with India’s fraud-prevention goals ●​ Operational breakdowns for businesses using multi-device workflows, cloud APIs, and automated messaging systems ●​ User-level impact, particularly for travellers, NRIs, creators, remote workers, and enterprises dependent on desktop or web clients ●​ Gaps in the rationale, including misalignment between stated fraud-prevention goals and actual cybercrime vectors 2 MEDIANAMA www.medianama.com ●​ Regulatory overreach, particularly the creeping classification of online platforms under telecom rules ●​ The need for a multi-stakeholder dialogue among technologists, legal experts, social media platforms, SMEs, and policy advisors to outline practical and proportional alternatives ●​ Documentation of key insights for broader public discourse and policymaker engagement This was an open discussion with participants from organisations including: Bharti Airtel, X (formerly Twitter), Broadband India Forum, Com First, Xiaomi, Cyber Cafe Association of India (CCAOI), Deloitte, Dvara, Info Edge, Bitscore, HasGeek, CUTS, Asia Society, Deep Strat, India Future Foundation, SFLC, Esya Centre, Inshorts, Bureau, ISOC, Sinch, and others.

MediaNama hosted this discussion with support from Meta and Snap. The community partners for the event were the Internet Freedom Foundation (IFF), Software Freedom Law Centre (SFLC.in), and the Centre for Internet and Society. The following document captures and summarises the key points raised during the discussion. You can also view a recording of the discussion on our YouTube channel.

3 MEDIANAMA www.medianama.com Index Index Regulatory Obligations Under the SIM Binding Directive: The scope of the SIM Binding: The Rationale Behind the SIM Binding Directive: Key Points from the Discussion: 1. What is SIM Binding, and why is it Difficult to implement: 2. SIM Binding Can’t Solve the Cybercrime Problems: 3. The Financial Costs of SIM Binding and Who Will Bear Them: 4. Telecom-Based Obligations and Risk Indicators as Alternatives to SIM Binding: 5.

Should DoT even regulate the Internet through TIUEs? Reference Materials: 4 MEDIANAMA www.medianama.com Executive Summary: India’s Department of Telecommunications (DoT) has issued a directive mandating continuous SIM–device binding for app-based messaging platforms that use a mobile number as a user identifier. This requires the physical presence of a SIM card for continued access to certain services offered by these applications.

The directive represents a significant shift in regulatory approach and has far-reaching implications for how internet-based communication platforms operate. During MediaNama’s discussion on the Impact of SIM Binding on Social Media, participants highlighted that the OS-level access required for continuous SIM verification is restricted under current iOS and Android privacy-oriented frameworks. As a result, OTP-based verification remains the only technically feasible and implementable option.

However, several speakers noted that enforcing continuous OTP-based SIM binding would impose recurring costs on users, banks, and platforms, while disrupting user experience, multi-device access, automation, and enterprise messaging workflows. Speakers also questioned whether SIM binding would effectively reduce cybercrime.

Many pointed out that prevalent cyber fraud mechanisms, such as phishing, OTP theft, caller ID spoofing, SIM swaps, virtual numbers, international SIMs, eSIMs, and email-based scams, would remain unaffected. In this context, participants cautioned that the directive risks displacing fraud rather than addressing its underlying causes. The discussion further raised concerns around regulatory scope and jurisdiction.

Participants questioned the DoT’s use of telecom cybersecurity rules to regulate internet-based applications traditionally governed under the Information Technology Act. Speakers highlighted ambiguities around legislative competence, the expanding scope of the Telecommunication Identifier User Entities (TIUEs) framework, and the absence of a clear and intelligible criterion for platform selection and enforcement.

Addressing these concerns, speakers outlined alternative approaches to fraud mitigation using telecom-based, risk-driven frameworks. These approaches emphasise probabilistic risk indicators, network-level signals, and targeted interventions in high-risk scenarios, rather than continuous, user-facing verification.

Participants argued that such models would better align fraud-prevention objectives with privacy, proportionality, and operational feasibility, without the systemic costs and user friction associated with blanket SIM-binding mandates. 5 MEDIANAMA www.medianama.com Recommendations: 1.​ Maintain a clear regulatory boundary between telecom and the internet: Internet-based services should not fall within the scope of the Telecommunication Act, 2023.

Extending telecom regulations to these services solely because they use mobile numbers to identify users lacks a clear legal basis and sound rationale.​ 2.​ Clearly define the scope and applicability of TIUEs: The definition of Telecommunication Identifier User Entities (TIUEs) is drafted broadly enough to potentially encompass large parts of the internet ecosystem, including international platforms whose primary function is not communication.
Such an expansive scope risks regulatory overreach and raises serious concerns around jurisdiction, proportionality, and legislative competence, making it vulnerable to legal challenge.

There is also limited clarity on how the SIM-binding rules would be enforced in practice, which platforms are currently covered, and whether additional platforms may already fall under the directive.​ 3.​ Make public consultation compulsory before issuing far-reaching orders like SIM binding: Orders that mandate changes to how app-based communication services authenticate users should be preceded by public consultation, particularly when such measures have direct implications for technical feasibility, costs, and user experience.

The discussion highlighted that the directive assumes the availability of OS-level SIM access, which is restricted under existing iOS and Android privacy frameworks—leaving OTP-based mechanisms as the only viable option and raising concerns about financial viability. Without consultation, such assumptions remain untested, and the resulting directions risk imposing disproportionate costs on users, banks, and platforms, while failing to account for alternative, less disruptive approaches.

A consultative process would allow these constraints and trade-offs to be examined before issuing binding directions with wide-ranging impact.​ 4.​ The government should adopt the least intrusive regulatory mechanisms: Regulatory interventions aimed at addressing cybercrime should prioritise mechanisms that achieve the intended objective while minimising disruption to users and platforms.

The discussion highlighted that continuous, user-facing verification measures introduce repeated friction, increase costs, and degrade user experience, particularly for services reliant on persistent logins, multi-device access, or safety-critical workflows.

Where alternative approaches exist, such as risk-based 6 MEDIANAMA www.medianama.com assessments, network-level signals, or targeted interventions triggered by suspicious activity, these should be preferred over blanket requirements that affect all users irrespective of risk.

Adopting the least intrusive mechanisms would help ensure proportionality, preserve usability, and reduce unintended consequences while still enabling effective responses to genuine threats.​ 5.​ Utilise probabilistic risk indicators for cybercrime prevention: Cybercrime prevention frameworks should rely on probabilistic risk indicators that can adapt to evolving threat models and user behaviours, rather than fixed, deterministic rules.

Risk signals such as SIM age, usage patterns, and anomalous activity provide a more flexible and effective basis for identifying suspicious behaviour, enabling targeted interventions where risk is highest without imposing uniform restrictions on all users.​ 6.​ Develop a composite risk index using telecom data and share it with applications: Telecom operators already possess multiple data points relevant to fraud detection, including the age of a mobile number, KYC modification history, revocation status, and location-change patterns.

Aggregating these signals into a composite risk index and making it available to applications would allow platforms to assess risk in real time and take proportionate action, without relying on continuous, user-facing verification or disrupting normal usage.​ 7.​ Utilise GSMA Connect (CAMARA) APIs for silent, low-friction verification: Where verification is necessary, network-level APIs such as GSMA Connect (CAMARA) should be used to enable silent, background checks that do not rely on repeated SMS-based OTPs.

These mechanisms allow telecom operators to share SIM- or risk-related signals with applications in a low-friction manner, reducing costs, improving security, and avoiding disruption to legitimate users’ access and workflows. 7 MEDIANAMA www.medianama.com Introduction: The SIM binding directive marks a significant expansion in how the Department of Telecommunications (DoT) is attempting to apply telecom regulatory frameworks to internet-based applications.

Under the Telecommunication Cybersecurity Rules, 2024, and through the definition of Telecommunication Identifier User Entities (TIUEs), the DoT has extended regulatory obligations beyond licensed telecom operators to a wide range of app-based services that use mobile phone numbers to identify users.

This approach effectively brings internet-based communication services within the ambit of telecom regulation, even though such platforms have historically been governed under the Information Technology Act and related frameworks. The reliance on mobile numbers as an identifying factor becomes the primary trigger for regulatory inclusion, blurring the boundary between telecom services and over-the-top (OTT) applications.

As a result, platforms whose core function is not telecommunications, but which incorporate communication or messaging features, could also fall within scope. While the measure is positioned as a response to cyber fraud arising from the misuse of SIM cards, it does not directly address the most prevalent fraud mechanisms in India, such as phishing, caller ID spoofing, OTP theft, SIM swaps, and VoIP-based impersonation.

Instead, the directive introduces additional friction for legitimate users, disrupting persistent login sessions that may be essential for business messaging and Customer Relationship Management (CRM) operations, and complicating access for travellers, NRIs, and users reliant on desktop or web-based clients.

Regulatory Obligations Under the SIM Binding Directive: Under the SIM binding directive issued by the Department of Telecommunications (DoT), entities classified as Telecommunication Identifier User Entities (TIUEs) and providing app-based communication services using mobile numbers for user identification are required to ensure continuous SIM linkage to the user’s account.

This obligation mandates that applications must stop delivering services unless an active, user-verified SIM linked to the account is present in the device. In addition to enforcing continuous SIM binding, the directive places specific requirements on how these services operate across devices. Applications offering web-based or multi-device access are required to periodically log users out, no later than every six hours, and provide a QR-code-based mechanism for relinking accounts.

These measures are intended to ensure that access to app-based communication services remains tied to the presence of the authenticated SIM in the device. 8 MEDIANAMA www.medianama.com MediaNama’s earlier reporting has highlighted that SIM binding is technically infeasible at the operating system (OS) level. As a result, compliance is expected to rely on application-level or network-level mechanisms rather than direct OS-level SIM access.

The directive does not prescribe a specific technical implementation, leaving TIUEs responsible for determining how to meet these requirements while remaining compliant with existing platform constraints and privacy safeguards.

The scope of the SIM Binding: The SIM binding directive issued by the Department of Telecommunications (DoT) derives its scope from the Telecommunication Cybersecurity Rules, 2024, as amended, which introduced a new regulatory category known as Telecommunication Identifier User Entities (TIUEs). TIUEs are defined as entities, other than licensed telecom operators, that use telecommunication identifiers, such as mobile numbers, for identifying users, or for provisioning or delivering services.

This definition significantly expands the regulatory perimeter beyond traditional telecom services to include a wide range of internet-based platforms that rely on mobile numbers at any stage of service delivery.

Under the directions issued pursuant to these rules, TIUEs providing app-based communication services that use mobile numbers for user identification or service delivery are required to ensure that their services remain continuously linked to the SIM card associated with that mobile number. The directions further mandate that such services must not function without the presence of the specific, active SIM installed in the device.

In addition, TIUEs offering web-based access are required to log users out periodically, no later than every six hours, and provide a QR-code-based mechanism for re-linking accounts. While the DoT has explicitly issued directions to platforms such as WhatsApp, Telegram, Signal, Arattai, Snapchat, ShareChat, JioChat, and Josh, MediaNama’s reporting has highlighted concerns that the underlying definition of TIUEs is sufficiently broad to potentially cover a much wider set of services.

Early reactions to the rules noted that, as drafted, the TIUE framework could extend to any entity that uses mobile numbers to identify users or deliver services, including platforms whose primary function is not messaging.

9 MEDIANAMA www.medianama.com The Rationale Behind the SIM Binding Directive: According to the Department of Telecommunications (DoT), the SIM binding directive is intended to address cybersecurity vulnerabilities arising from app-based communication services and to prevent cybercrime.

In the government’s assessment, the current design allows misuse of mobile-number-linked SIMs from outside India on these TIUE platforms, creating barriers for investigation and enforcement in cases of cyber-related crime and fraud. As outlined in the directions and supported by industry bodies, the DoT appears to observe that the binding between a subscriber’s mobile number and an app-based communication service typically occurs only once, during the initial installation and verification stage.

After this point, many applications continue to operate independently on the device, even if the SIM card is later removed, replaced, or deactivated. The government argues that this weakens traceability, as the absence of an active SIM limits access to verifiable telecom-based metadata such as call records, cell-tower location information, and carrier logs. This rationale aligns with the position articulated by the Cellular Operators Association of India (COAI).

It has argued that the lack of persistent SIM binding enables app-based communication services to be misused for criminal activity without a reliable link to the user’s location or telecom network. COAI has suggested that maintaining continuous SIM–device linkage would ensure that such communication services cannot operate without the authenticated SIM present in the device, thereby enabling traceability between the user, the number, and the device.

From the government’s perspective, persistent SIM binding is framed as a preventive measure aimed at reducing spam, fraud, and financial crimes by acting as a deterrent against the misuse of over-the-top communication platforms. The DoT has positioned this approach as one that could provide relief not only to telecom service providers, but also to app-based platforms, by strengthening accountability at the identifier level. 10 MEDIANAMA www.medianama.com Key Points from the Discussion: 1.

What is SIM Binding, and why is it Difficult to implement: The Department of Telecommunications (DoT) defines SIM binding as a permanent lock between an application and a specific SIM card used to identify the user. This makes the SIM’s continuous presence a precondition for accessing the app’s services. Any break in this linkage, whether due to SIM removal, swapping, or inactivity, would result in the discontinuation of services.

If SIM binding is implemented, users cannot use the app without an active SIM in the device: “ SIM binding is basically verifying that the device on which you are using an app actually has the SIM that is linked to that app. This means the app, whether it is a banking app, a UPI app, or any other app, must have a certain number linked to it, because we use numbers to link apps. If that linkage breaks, then what the DoT wants is for the app not to work.

This means that if, in some way, the linkage breaks because the SIM has been taken out, the app should not work. That is the intent.”​ — Parag Kar, former Vice President (VP) at Qualcomm for Government Affairs (India and South Asia ​ There are two practical ways to implement the SIM Binding: “See, the practical implementation of SIM binding can be done in two ways. One is the app reading the SIM directly at the OS level.
You have two types of operating systems: one is iOS and the other is Android.

So, if the app has access to read the SIM information directly from the OS, then it can decide whether the SIM is present or not, because the data is right there on the phone itself.” ​ — Parag Kar, former Vice President (VP) at Qualcomm for Government Affairs (India and South Asia) ​ However, several speakers pointed out that absolute SIM binding at the OS level is practically infeasible, given existing operating system restrictions on SIM access and the privacy safeguards enforced by both iOS and Android.​ ​ OTP-Based Mechanism Is the Only Viable Implementation Path: “Another way to do it is to poll a network provider or the bank and let the bank decide where that OTP has originated, because the app has to originate an OTP.

The 11 MEDIANAMA www.medianama.com OTP will have metadata, which includes the telephone number, and the telephone number will have device information also attached to it. So it will go to the bank, and the bank will say, ‘Okay, now I understand that this device has this telephone number and this device has this trusted ID.’ So that binding gets created on the server, not on the device.

It is created on the bank server or the OTP server, and that binding remains there for the time when the transaction is happening. Now, if the bank somehow becomes suspicious about the binding changing on the device, then it has to re-initiate the OTP.”— Parag Kar, former Vice President (VP) at Qualcomm for Government Affairs (India and South Asia OS-Level SIM Binding Verification Is Impossible:​ “Without that OTP, you cannot verify whether the SIM is present on the phone or not.

Now, the problem is that the first case I mentioned is not allowed by the iOS system. The iOS system prevented it long back. Android used to allow it in earlier versions, but they are now preventing it. Why are they preventing it? Because it is a privacy issue. There are various kinds of apps that are going to use information to sell their products.” ​ ​ Now, the problem is, how many OTPs you will send and when you will send them. Will you send them every minute? Will you send them every day?
Will you send them every year? What is the event that will trigger the sending of that OTP? That is point number one.

You have to understand that without that OTP being sent– with the metadata of the device as well as the mobile number sent to the bank– there cannot be any binding, and the binding happens on the bank [app] server.​ —Parag Kar, former Vice President (VP) at Qualcomm for Government Affairs (India and South Asia) ​ The challenge then becomes determining how frequently OTPs would need to be sent. Would they be sent every minute, every day, or every year?

What event would trigger an OTP request?​ You have to understand that without that OTP being sent– with the metadata of the device as well as the mobile number sent to the bank– there cannot be any binding, and the binding happens on the bank [app] server.​ —Parag Kar, former Vice President (VP) at Qualcomm for Government Affairs (India and South Asia) Misconception: UPI and Banking Apps Already Implement SIM Binding: Anand Venkatanarayanan clarified the misconception that banking and UPI applications currently implement SIM binding.

Instead, these systems rely on device-level verification mechanisms, not SIM binding. 12 MEDIANAMA www.medianama.com “Actually, that is the entire misconstruction about UPI supporting SIM binding. UPI actually supports device binding with SendSMS. Apple had to invent a whole new API, which they internally call the device binding API, in order to support UPI on iOS. In fact, most UPI apps will not work if you are not using iOS 17, because they all use that SendSMS API.

Apps that use the SendSMS API have to be manually verified, must be given entitlements, and must be certified by NPCI. Only those apps get access to that API, not just any app.” —Anand Venkatanarayanan, Co-Founder, Deepstrat Messaging Apps Lack Incentives to Enforce SIM Binding​ One speaker pointed out that communication-based applications lack strong incentives to impose SIM binding, which introduces significant user friction.

“Most banking apps do some form of risk detection and determine that if the risk crosses a certain threshold—if the SIM is being switched off or not present—then they will ask the user to re-trigger the entire verification process. If you go down the path of device binding with SIM presence detection, the user experience for any app is going to be disastrous.

Banks can force users to go through that friction because there is money involved, liability exists, and there are regulatory requirements. But if you apply the same logic to messaging apps, what exactly is the liability? What exactly is the regulation? Most of them are free services. If you start implementing this there, you would suddenly find most apps becoming unusable.” —Anand Venkatanarayanan, Co-Founder, Deepstrat ​ ​ 2.

SIM Binding Can’t Solve the Cybercrime Problems: Several speakers noted that even if the SIM binding mandate were implemented, it would not achieve the broader objective of preventing or significantly reducing overall cybercrime. SIM binding won’t stop fraudsters from exploiting other channels: “Let’s assume that all messaging apps do this precisely as mandated by the DoT.

I would say it would probably make no difference for fraudsters, because they would then go back to using different types of messengers. For instance, one of the most common methods they used during the COVID period was email—specifically, email links.” — Anand Venkatanarayanan, Co-Founder, Deepstrat. 13 MEDIANAMA www.medianama.com ​ Use of international SIMs remains a key loophole in cyber fraud: “It can only be implemented for Indian mobile numbers, right?

So if someone is using international numbers, we have seen a lot of scams happening using international numbers: +1, +3, +9, whatever it is, what is going to stop fraudsters from using these international numbers? It is very easy today to get an international number. There are things called virtual numbers, where I can just buy these virtual numbers and use them, or eSIMs. These days, eSIMs are very popular.

I can activate eSIMs, use international eSIMs, and use those virtual numbers to commit fraud. It is not solving the problem in the way it is being advertised. It will not stop the frauds that are happening.

This [SIM mandate] is not a silver bullet.”— Venkata Satish Guttula, Cyber Security Consultant, Allied Boston Consultants ​ OTP-based verification also has inherent vulnerabilities Explaining the limitations of OTP-based verification, one of the speakers noted that OTPs can also be spoofed, leaving residual vulnerabilities and continued exposure to cyber risks.

“So if there is a way where I can spoof that [OTP] message which the app is reading, then I can spoof it without the phone knowing that the SIM card is there. When I was testing my UPI app, it asked me to disconnect the Wi-Fi because it wanted to connect to the mobile internet. But the thing is, the SIM card I was using to register UPI was a different SIM card, and the internet was from a different provider.

Still, it could not know that I was using a different medium for the internet and a different medium to send a message.” — Venkata Satish Guttula, Cyber Security Consultant, Allied Boston Consultants. “So, just for people who are really clueless about this, the specific spoofing problem actually existed in UPI 1.0. In fact, UPI 2.0 came about only because of that specific problem.

They finally figured out that the only way to do device binding correctly with SendSMS was to make Apple provide a very specific API only for UPI apps. So iOS 17 had to introduce a very specific new API that was only usable and accessible to UPI apps certified and vetted by NPCI. It was that complicated and messy. So if you go back and think about what SIM binding even is, which is the fundamental question I have always asked, you had device binding with SendSMS, custom SendSMS.
That is all you ever had, even in India.”—Anand Venkatanarayanan, Co-Founder, Deepstrat 14 MEDIANAMA www.medianama.com ​ ​ 3.

The Financial Costs of SIM Binding and Who Will Bear Them: While it is clear that, in the absence of OS-level access to SIM data on either iOS or Android, OTP-based verification remains the only technically feasible mechanism for SIM binding, this raises a key question: who will bear the cost of the repeated OTPs that app providers must send to ensure that the SIM remains bound to a specific device?

The goal of the OTP-based SIM binding appears to be yet another revenue stream to telecom operators: “Who is going to bear the cost of the OTP? Is the user going to bear it, or the app provider, or the bank? Who is going to bear it? Because when an OTP is sent, the user is initiating the OTP. You might have seen that whenever an OTP is sent, the app tells you that there will be a cost involved when the OTP is sent. Are you willing to bear the cost?

So you have to bear the cost, maybe one time, two times, how many times? You have to bear the cost of the OTP because there is no way to access the iOS system or the Android system to verify the SIM” — Anand Venkatanarayanan, Co-Founder, Deepstrat The telecom network risks becoming an SMS carrier “The reason the mobile operator is pushing for SIM binding is for commercial reasons. They also know that it is not practically possible to implement. They want to make money.

That is why they are going ahead and saying that everybody should send SMS, and everybody should be sending SMS all the time. The whole network is going to become an SMS carrier now, right? Because more SMS means more money, both ways.

That is why they are doing it.” — Anand Venkatanarayanan, Co-Founder, Deepstrat Placing the discussion in a broader regulatory context, one of the speakers pointed to the long-standing friction between telecom operators and internet-based services over the past decade. This tension has played out internationally as well.

For instance, during the World Conference on International Telecommunications (WCIT) in Dubai, sections of the telecom industry pushed for internet services to be brought within the regulatory framework of the International Telecommunication Union (ITU). Similar debates have emerged in Europe, where telecom industry bodies, including the European Telecommunications Network Operators’ Association, have advanced proposals such as the so-called “sender-pays” or network contribution principle.

The speaker argued 15 MEDIANAMA www.medianama.com that the current approach to SIM binding must be viewed against this backdrop of recurring attempts by telecom operators to extend regulatory control over internet services. Telecom operators’ intent to extract additional revenue from internet-dependent applications: “So essentially, the idea was that, for example, if you are using YouTube and consuming its content, then YouTube should pay for it. That was the idea.

We have seen this friction largely because of revenue-related issues, such as revenue sharing and regulation. There has been an argument that telecom operators are strictly regulated, whereas internet services operate under a light-touch regulatory framework. If you move from the international context to the Indian context, this proposal has again come from telecom operators. The argument seems to be that there should be some regulation of services that, in a way, compete with telecom services.
WhatsApp, for example, competes with traditional voice and other communication services.

The broader question, then, is whether, under the Telecommunications Act, which is essentially meant to regulate telecommunications, spectrum, and related aspects, it is even possible to frame such rules or regulations to control information services or over-the-top (OTT) services like WhatsApp.” — Prasanth Sugathan, Legal Director, SFLC.in Explaining how SIM binding and OTP-based verification work in practice, the speaker outlined why banks continue to rely on multiple authentication layers to verify both the device and the user, and why repeatedly breaking and re-establishing this linkage would significantly increase costs for users, banks, and platforms, while degrading the overall user experience.​ ​ ​ SIM binding will increase costs for both users and app providers “So, the problem is that whenever this [verification or binding] association is broken, the entire process has to be repeated again and again.

There is a cost involved for the user, and there is a cost involved for the bank in sending the OTP. If the DoT’s order has to be implemented on a continuous basis, the user’s mobile bill is going to increase from the current level to a potentially disproportionate level. At the same time, the bills for WhatsApp or any other entity that has to keep sending SMS will also rise, making the process completely financially unviable.

It will not only destroy the user experience but also make the system financially unviable.”​ — Parag Kar, former Vice President (VP) at Qualcomm for Government Affairs (India and South Asia) 16 MEDIANAMA www.medianama.com ​ 4.

Telecom-Based Obligations and Risk Indicators as Alternatives to SIM Binding: Building on the technical and economic concerns discussed earlier, speakers noted that OS-level SIM binding is technically infeasible, while OTP-based SIM binding raises serious questions of financial viability. Against this backdrop, one of the speakers outlined a set of less invasive, privacy-focused alternatives to SIM binding as it is currently conceived.

Utilise GSMA’s CAMARA API protocol as an alternative to SIM binding: “You can send a different type of message that will not have that friction, and it can be sent silently. Therefore, there is a protocol called the CAMARA API protocol—GSMA Connect. If the GSMA Connect protocol keeps polling to check whether a SIM is bound or not, it does not serve any purpose. You have to do it in a very discrete and risk-mitigation way. Identify a group of people who are miscreants.

Do not tell them that you are polling them. The network knows that if there is risky behaviour—if your location is here and suddenly, after 1,000 miles, your location changes. Who will know all these things? The mobile operator will know.

That is the reason why, internationally, nobody is doing all this SIM binding.” — Parag Kar, former Vice President (VP) at Qualcomm for Government Affairs (India and South Asia) ​ Explaining the security capabilities already available at the network level, Parag highlighted how mobile operators can use physical-layer data and 5G features without relying on GPS or the public internet.​ ​ Telecom networks have more information than the app providers: “The mobile operator, at the physical layer, has loads of information.

It does not have to go through GPS. It knows the Base Transceiver Station (BTS) ID from where the mobile actually originated. If the location suddenly changes, it can immediately tell. Now, because of the information that the mobile operator has, this information can be put at a central location and served to high-risk entities like banks and others to make their systems more secure. Similarly, if you look at 5G, it has a system called network-level slicing.

It gives an app a specific path, a logical path, which does not go through the public internet.” — Parag Kar, former Vice President (VP) at Qualcomm for Government Affairs (India and South Asia) 17 MEDIANAMA www.medianama.com However, while network slicing was offered as a potential solution, Nikhil Pahwa responded by stating that such an approach would violate net neutrality principles.

In addition, speakers argued that telecom-led cybercrime prevention should rely on probabilistic risk indicators that evolve over time in response to changing conditions and adversary behaviour, rather than fixed mechanisms such as SIM binding. Composite risk indices can be used to identify high-risk users: “A composite risk indicator can be constructed out of many pieces of data, and almost all those pieces of data are actually with the telecom operator.

For instance, one of the most common risk indicators in banking applications is the age of the SIM. How far has KYC been modified? When was it reviewed or put on the revocation list? You go down this path and figure out what the most common areas are. So you could take a whole bunch of this information and create a composite risk index and say whether this is a problematic user or not. Many banking applications use some version of a composite risk index, including some UPI apps.

It is perfectly possible to create a composite risk index without getting involved in SIM binding for cybercrime.”— Anand Venkatanarayanan, Co-Founder, Deepstrat He also illustrated how various fraud detection systems already rely on behavioural and network-level signals.

One speaker outlined commonly used risk identifiers such as the type and age of a mobile number, patterns of location change, and the extent of a user’s online presence, signals that are already widely used across financial and lending systems. ​ ​ 5. Should DoT even regulate the Internet through TIUEs?

Speakers pointed out that the current Telecommunication Identifier User Entities (TIUEs) framework under the Telecommunications (Telecom Cybersecurity) Amendment Rules, 2025, is overly broad and could potentially include a vast number of entities, including those using Internet Protocol (IP) addresses.

All number-using applications could fall under TIUEs: “If an entity is using a telecom identifier—the classic example being a mobile number—for the identification of the customer or for the delivery or provisioning of a service, then that entity is a TIUE as defined under the regulation. I’ll give an example. Let’s take WhatsApp or Signal. How is WhatsApp or Signal directing communication to you? How is the user identified on that platform? It is using the number.

So when Kamya is messaging Nikhil, Kamya’s number is shown against 18 MEDIANAMA www.medianama.com Kamya, Nikhil’s number is shown against Nikhil, and WhatsApp, to some extent, is relying on these numbers as identifiers. Users on the platform can search using these numbers and message each other using these numbers.

That is the framework within which this regulation has been issued by the government.” — Nikhil Narendran, Partner, TMT Practice, Trilegal However, speakers argued that the regulation should apply only to apps that provide communication services and use mobile phone numbers as the primary user identifier. SIM binding applies to number-based communication apps, not all TIUEs: “The [SIM Binding] rules, per se, talk about TIUEs not just from the perspective of communication.

They are also broad enough that if other telecom identifiers, for instance, an IP address, are used to deliver some other kind of service, that could also get covered under the regulations. It is pretty broad, right?

But if you look specifically at the SIM binding regulations, the scope seems to be narrower, in the sense that if you are using an identifier like a number and delivering an app-based communication service, then it comes under the ambit of the directions issued.” — Nikhil Narendran, Partner, TMT Practice, Trilegal Countering this argument, Nikhil Pahwa cited Bumble, a dating app, as an example of a TIUE that also uses text-based communication features and mobile numbers to identify users.

He questioned why notices were issued only to a limited set of platforms when similar cyber scams could occur on other services, including dating apps.​ ​ No intelligible criteria to categorise TIUEs: “There is no discernible, intelligible criteria defined under the law, the rules, or the directive based on which the eight companies have been selected to be served notices.”— Nikhil Pahwa, Founder and Editor, MediaNama.

​ Later, he also commented on the arbitrary nature in which this directive was issued and proposed that public and stakeholder consultations should be the ideal way to bring the regulations which can have wider impact.

Conduct public consultations to understand the issues better: ““So, I think it would be useful if the DoT actually opened up to feedback and conducted public consultations so that it understands the situation and the impact better before issuing such far-reaching orders,” — Nikhil Pahwa, Founder and Editor, MediaNama.
