PML Rules, 2005 R. 9(12) Client Due Diligence Page 64 of 98 THE BHARATIYA NAGARIK SURAKSHA SANHITA, 2023 I. Section 94: Summons to produce document or other thing.

(1) Whenever any Court or any officer in charge of a police station considers that the production of any document, electronic communication, including communication devices, which is likely to contain digital evidence or other thing is necessary or desirable for the purposes of any investigation, inquiry, trial or other proceeding under this Sanhita by or before such Court or officer, such Court may issue a summons or such officer may, by a written order, either in physical form or in electronic form, require the person in whose possession or power such document or thing is believed to be, to attend and produce it, or to produce it, at the time and place stated in the summons or order.

(2) Any person required under this Section merely to produce a document, or other thing, shall be deemed to have complied with the requisition if he causes such document or thing to be produced instead of attending personally to produce the same.

(3) Nothing in this Section shall be deemed— (a) to affect Sections 129 and 130 of the Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023 or the Bankers' Books Evidence Act, 1891; or (b) to apply to a letter, postcard, or other document or any parcel or thing in the custody of the postal authority. II.

Section 106: Power of police officer to seize certain property (1) Any police officer may seize any property which may be alleged or suspected to have been stolen, or which may be found under circumstances which create suspicion of the commission of any offence. Page 65 of 98 (2) Such police officer, if subordinate to the officer in charge of a police station, shall forthwith report the seizure to that officer.

(3) Every police officer acting under sub-section (1) shall forthwith report the seizure to the Magistrate having jurisdiction and where the property seized is such that it cannot be conveniently transported to the Court, or where there is difficulty in securing proper accommodation for the custody of such property, or where the continued retention of the property in police custody may not be considered necessary for the purpose of investigation, he may give custody thereof to any person on his executing a bond undertaking to produce the property before the Court as and when required and to give effect to the further orders of the Court as to the disposal of the same: Provided that where the property seized under sub-Section (1) is subject to speedy and natural decay and if the person entitled to the possession of such property is unknown or absent and the value of such property is less than five hundred rupees, it may forthwith be sold by auction under the orders of the Superintendent of Police and the provisions of Sections 503 and 504 shall, as nearly as may be practicable, apply to the net proceeds of such sale.

III. Section 168: Police to prevent cognizable offences. Every police officer may interpose for the purpose of preventing, and shall, to the best of his ability, prevent, the commission of any cognizable offence. IV. Section 193: Report of police officer on completion of investigation. (1) Every investigation under this Chapter shall be completed without unnecessary delay.

Page 66 of 98 (2) The investigation in relation to an offence under sections 64, 65, 66, 67, 68, 70, 71 of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 or under sections 4, 6, 8 or section 10 of the Protection of Children from Sexual Offences Act, 2012 shall be completed within two months from the date on which the information was recorded by the officer in charge of the police station.

(3) (i) As soon as the investigation is completed, the officer in charge of the police station shall forward, including through electronic communication to a Magistrate empowered to take cognizance of the offence on a police report, a report in the form as the State Government may, by rules provide, stating— (a) the names of the parties; (b) the nature of the information; (c) the names of the persons who appear to be acquainted with the circumstances of the case; (d) whether any offence appears to have been committed and, if so, by whom; (e) whether the accused has been arrested; (f) whether the accused has been released on his bond or bail bond; (g) whether the accused has been forwarded in custody under section 190; (h) whether the report of medical examination of the woman has been attached where investigation relates to an offence under sections 64, 65, 66, 67, 68, 70 or section 71 of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023; (i) the sequence of custody in case of electronic device; Page 67 of 98 (ii) the police officer shall, within a period of ninety days, inform the progress of the investigation by any means including through electronic communication to the informant or the victim; (iii) the officer shall also communicate, in such manner as the State Government may, by rules, provide, the action taken by him, to the person, if any, by whom the information relating to the commission of the offence was first given.

(4) Where a superior officer of police has been appointed under section 177, the report shall, in any case in which the State Government by general or special order so directs, be submitted through that officer, and he may, pending the orders of the Magistrate, direct the officer in charge of the police station to make further investigation.

(5) Whenever it appears from a report forwarded under this section that the accused has been released on his bond or bail bond, the Magistrate shall make such order for the discharge of such bond or bail bond or otherwise as he thinks fit.

(6) When such report is in respect of a case to which section 190 applies, the police officer shall forward to the Magistrate along with the report— (a) all documents or relevant extracts thereof on which the prosecution proposes to rely other than those already sent to the Magistrate during investigation; (b) the statements recorded under section 180 of all the persons whom the prosecution proposes to examine as its witnesses.

(7) If the police officer is of opinion that any part of any such statement is not relevant to the subject matter of the proceedings or that its disclosure to the accused is not essential in the interests of justice and Page 68 of 98 is inexpedient in the public interest, he shall indicate that part of the statement and append a note requesting the Magistrate to exclude that part from the copies to be granted to the accused and stating his reasons for making such request.

(8) Subject to the provisions contained in sub-section (7), the police officer investigating the case shall also submit such number of copies of the police report along with other documents duly indexed to the Magistrate for supply to the accused as required under section 230: Provided that supply of report and other documents by electronic communication shall be considered as duly served.

(9) Nothing in this section shall be deemed to preclude further investigation in respect of an offence after a report under sub-section (3) has been forwarded to the Magistrate and, where upon such investigation, the officer in charge of the police station obtains further evidence, oral or documentary, he shall forward to the Magistrate a further report or reports regarding such evidence in the form as the State Government may, by rules, provide; and the provisions of sub- sections (3) to (8) shall, as far as may be, apply in relation to such report or reports as they apply in relation to a report forwarded under sub-section (3): Provided that further investigation during the trial may be conducted with the permission of the Court trying the case and the same shall be completed within a period of ninety days which may be extended with the permission of the Court.
Page 69 of 98 V. Section 497: Order for custody and disposal of property pending trial in certain cases.

(1) When any property is produced before any Criminal Court or the Magistrate empowered to take cognizance or commit the case for trial during any investigation, inquiry or trial, the Court or the Magistrate may make such order as it thinks fit for the proper custody of such property pending the conclusion of the investigation, inquiry or trial, and, if the property is subject to speedy and natural decay, or if it is otherwise expedient so to do, the Court or the Magistrate may, after recording such evidence as it thinks necessary, order it to be sold or otherwise disposed of.

Explanation. —For the purposes of this Section, "property" includes— (a) property of any kind or document which is produced before the Court or which is in its custody; (b) any property regarding which an offence appears to have been committed or which appears to have been used for the commission of any offence.

(2) The Court or the Magistrate shall, within a period of fourteen days from the production of the property referred to in sub-Section (1) before it, prepare a statement of such property containing its description in such form and manner as the State Government may, by rules, provide. (3) The Court or the Magistrate shall cause to be taken the photograph and if necessary, video graph on mobile phone or any electronic media, of the property referred to in sub-Section (1).

(4) The statement prepared under sub-Section (2) and the photograph or the videography taken under sub-Section (3) shall be used as evidence in any inquiry, trial or other proceeding under the Sanhita.

Page 70 of 98 (5) The Court or the Magistrate shall, within a period of thirty days after the statement has been prepared under sub-Section (2) and the photograph or the videography has been taken under sub- Section (3), order the disposal, destruction, confiscation or delivery of the property in the manner specified hereinafter. VI. Section 503: Procedure by police upon seizure of property.

(1) Whenever the seizure of property by any police officer is reported to a Magistrate under the provisions of this Sanhita, and such property is not produced before a Criminal Court during an inquiry or trial, the Magistrate may make such order as he thinks fit respecting the disposal of such property or the delivery of such property to the person entitled to the possession thereof, or if such person cannot be ascertained, respecting the custody and production of such property.

(2) If the person so entitled is known, the Magistrate may order the property to be delivered to him on such conditions (if any) as the Magistrate thinks fit and if such person is unknown, the Magistrate may detain it and shall, in such case, issue a proclamation specifying the articles of which such property consists, and requiring any person who may have a claim thereto, to appear before him and establish his claim within six months from the date of such proclamation.

Page 74 of 98 THE PREVENTION OF MONEY-LAUNDERING ACT, 2002 (PMLA) Section 12AA – Enhanced Due Diligence (1) Every reporting entity shall, prior to the commencement of each specified transaction, —— (a) Verify the identity of the clients undertaking such specified transaction by authentication under the Aadhaar (Targeted Delivery of Financial and Other Subsidies, Benefits and Services) Act, 2016 in such manner and subject to such conditions, as may be prescribed: Provided that where verification requires authentication of a person who is not entitled to obtain an Aadhaar number under the provisions of the said Act, verification to authenticate the identity of the client undertaking such specified transaction shall be carried out by such other process or mode, as may be prescribed; (b) take additional steps to examine the ownership and financial position, including sources of funds of the client, in such manner as may be prescribed; (c) take additional steps as may be prescribed to record the purpose behind conducting the specified transaction and the intended nature of the relationship between the transaction parties.
(2) Where the client fails to fulfil the conditions laid down under sub- section (1), the reporting entity shall not allow the specified transaction to be carried out.

Page 75 of 98 (3) Where any specified transaction or series of specified transactions undertaken by a client is considered suspicious or likely to involve proceeds of crime, the reporting entity shall increase the future monitoring of the business relationship with the client, including greater scrutiny or transactions in such manner as may be prescribed.

(4) The information obtained while applying the enhanced due diligence measures under sub-section (1) shall be maintained for a period of five years from the date of transaction between a client and the reporting entity. Explanation. —For the purposes of this section, "specified transaction" means—.

(a) any withdrawal or deposit in cash, exceeding such amount; (b) any transaction in foreign exchange, exceeding such amount; (c) any transaction in any high value imports or remittances; (d) such other transaction or class of transactions, in the interest of revenue or where there is a high risk or money- laundering or terrorist financing, as may be prescribed. Page 76 of 98 Prevention of Money-Laundering (Maintenance of Records) Rules, 2005 Rule 9(12).

Client Due Diligence: (i) Every reporting entity shall exercise ongoing due diligence with respect to the business relationship with every client and closely examine the transactions in order to ensure that they are consistent with their knowledge of the client, his business and risk profile and where necessary, the source of funds.

(ii) When there are suspicions of money laundering or financing of the activities relating to terrorism or where there are doubts about the adequacy or veracity of previously obtained client identification data, the reporting entity shall review the due diligence measures including verifying again the identity of the client and obtaining information on the purpose and intended nature of the business relationship, as the case may be.

(iii) The reporting entity shall apply client due diligence measures also to existing clients on the basis of materiality and risk, and conduct due diligence on such existing relationships at appropriate times or as may be specified by the regulator, taking into account whether and when client due diligence measures have previously been undertaken and the adequacy of data obtained , such that the information or data collected under client due diligence is kept up-to-date and relevant, particularly where there is high risk.

Not to insist for Registration of FIR- NCRP Complaints Page 78 of 98 JURISDICTIONAL COURT JUDGMENTS 1. Dr Sajeer v.

Reserve Bank of India [ 2023 KHC Online 661]: - In this case, the Hon’ble Kerala High Court passed judgment addressing blanket freezing of bank accounts during cybercrime investigations, especially where funds were received by unsuspecting merchants or individuals with no role in the predicate offence.

The Court held that freezes must be proportionate and confined only to the specific amounts indicated in police requisitions, rather than immobilizing entire accounts; it also required timely communication by investigating police to banks on whether a partial freeze needs continuation and for how long, to preserve business continuity and public confidence in digital payments. 2. The Hon’ble Supreme Court of India in case of “R. K. Dalmia vs.

Delhi Administration" (1962) has opined that the word “property” in the Indian Penal Code (now BNS), when used without qualification (as in Section 405 on criminal breach of trust), is not confined to “movable property” and must be given the widest meaning, encompassing tangible and intangible interests including choses in action and company funds in bank accounts but also intangible assets such as bank accounts, funds, and securities held by financial institutions. 3.
The Hon’ble Supreme Court in OPTO Circuits (India) Ltd. v.

Axis Bank held that freezing a bank account under Section 17 of the PMLA must strictly comply with the statute’s procedural safeguards: the authorized officer must have “reason to believe” based on information, record such reasons in writing, issue a Page 79 of 98 freezing order only where seizure is impracticable, promptly forward reasons and material to the Adjudicating Authority, and within 30 days file for continuation before the Adjudicating Authority; failing these steps vitiates the freezing, warranting de-freezing.

The Court clarified that bank accounts alleged to contain proceeds of crime are both “property” and “records,” so freezing them squarely attracts Section 17’s requirements, and reiterated the settled rule that when a statute prescribes a manner of doing an act, it must be done in that manner or not at all. 4. While considering the issue of whether ‘bank accounts’ fall within the scope of Section 102 of the CrPC, it was held by the Hon’ble Supreme Court in State of Maharashtra vs. Tapas D.

Neogy, that even bank accounts fall within the phrase ‘any property’ under Section 102 of the CrPC and could therefore be frozen by the investigating authorities, if found to have direct links with the commission of an offence. The property must have a connection with the commission of a crime.

For the purpose of Section 102 of the CrPC, the property must be either: a) Alleged or suspected to have been stolen; or b) Have a nexus between the property and the commission of the crime; or c) Therefore, investigating authorities can only freeze bank accounts if the deposit in the account is stolen money or the account is connected with an alleged offence which is under investigation. 5. The Hon’ble Delhi High Court in the case of Ms. Swaran Sabharwal v.

Commissioner of Police (1987 SCC OnLine Del 221; 1988 Cri LJ 241) held that police powers under Section 102 Page 80 of 98 CrPC to “seize” property and issue prohibitory orders over bank accounts can be exercised only when the property itself is found in circumstances creating a present suspicion that an offence has been committed; if the bank account is discovered after the offence and investigation has already commenced, mere linkage to an accused without material showing the account as the source of discovering the offence does not justify freezing, and such prohibitory orders are liable to be quashed.

The Court stressed procedural safeguards: prompt reporting to the Magistrate, and a clear nexus showing that the discovery of property leads to the discovery of the offence, not vice versa. 6. In “Teesta Atul Setalvad v. State of Gujarat” the petitioner pleaded that the continued seizure of her bank account by the police authority is not valid as it does not amount to be property and is not related to the crime, so her account should be defreezed.

The Court after analysing the case ruled that the bank account comes under the realm of Section 102 of CrPC and the same would be counted as property and freezing of bank account by police under Section 102 is valid. 7. Mohammed Saifullah vs. Reserve Bank of India and Others W.P.No.25631 of 2024: In the Mohammed Saifullah v.

Reserve Bank of India case, the Madras High Court ruled that freezing an entire bank account due to a cybercrime investigation—without specific justification on the amount or duration—violates fundamental rights related to livelihood and business. The court directed the bank to unfreeze Mohammed Saifullah’s account, which had been frozen due to an investigation involving cryptocurrency transactions. Despite the investigation's focus on a Page 81 of 98 specific amount (Rs.

2,48,835), the entire account balance of Rs. 9,69,580 had been blocked for over a year without adequate notification or explanation to Saifullah case. The court ordered that Saifullah’s account be reactivated, allowing him to access his funds, but with a hold of Rs. 2,50,000 to cover any potential future liabilities associated with the ongoing investigation.

This decision emphasizes that while authorities have the power to freeze accounts under investigation, it must be exercised responsibly and in a limited scope to avoid undue harm to account holders. 8. In the case of Shento Varghese v. Julfikar Husen and Ors 2024 INSC 407. (Judgment dated 13th May 2024), the Supreme Court examined i. What is the implication of non-reporting of the seizure forthwith to the jurisdictional Magistrate as provided under Section 102(3) Cr.P.C.? ii.

Does delayed reporting of the seizure to the Magistrate vitiate the seizure order altogether? In this case, the accused’s bank account was initially frozen on police orders, and although a seizure report was submitted to the Magistrate, it was not done immediately. The accused appealed to the High Court, which ruled in his favour on the grounds that the reporting was not timely.

On appeal, however, the Supreme Court clarified the meaning of "forthwith" in this context, interpreting it as “as soon as reasonably possible,” recognizing that procedural actions must be prompt but allowing for reasonable delays based on circumstances. The Court noted that unless a strict timeframe is prescribed, actions should be completed within a reasonable period without a rigid formula.

Thus, the Supreme Court held that the Page 82 of 98 delayed submission of the seizure report did not vitiate the police’s freezing order, emphasizing the need for flexibility in interpreting procedural timelines. 9. In the case of Rakesh P. Sheth and Others v. State (Crl. O.P. No.

19618 of 2016), the Hon’ble Madras High Court, clarified that police power to freeze bank accounts under Section 102 CrPC (now Section 106 BNSS) is well-settled; however, prior intimation to the account holder is not required at the stage of seizure, since advance notice would frustrate the investigation and enable dissipation of suspected proceeds, provided the action is backed by contemporaneous suspicion and is forthwith reported to the Magistrate as mandated by law.

At the same time, after freezing, the officer must inform the account holder of the factum of seizure so that alternate arrangements can be made for ongoing obligations, and the affected party may seek appropriate relief for de-freezing before the competent court 10. Madhu K.V. Sub Inspector of Police and others [2020 (5) KLT 483]: In this case, the practice of certain police officers of directing the freezing of accounts without reporting to the Magistrate concerned was deprecated.

As rightly observed in this judgment, the police officer acting under Section 102 Cr.P.C cannot be permitted to arrogate to himself an unregulated and unbridled power to freeze the bank account of a person on mere surmise and conjuncture, since such unguarded power may bring about drastic consequences affecting the right to privacy as well as reputation of the account holder. Page 83 of 98 11. The Hon’ble Supreme Court order in State Bank of India v. Pallabh Bhowmik & Ors. (SLP(C) No.

30677/2024; order dated 3 January 2025) dismissed SBI’s challenge to a Gauhati High Court direction to refund the amount lost in unauthorized online transactions, emphasizing that banks bear responsibility to protect customers from fraudulent transactions and should use available technology to detect and prevent such frauds.

The Bench noted that the customer had reported the fraud within 24 hours and reiterated that RBI’s July 6, 2017 circular on liability for unauthorized electronic transactions applies where no customer negligence is established, while also observing that customers must remain vigilant and not share OTPs; on the facts, there was no reason to disturb the High Court’s order.

This ruling has been widely reported as reinforcing bank liability for fraudulent withdrawals and strengthening consumer protection norms in digital banking, with commentary highlighting that banks must compensate victims where negligence is not attributable to the customer and must maintain robust security and monitoring systems. 12. Abdul Azeez v.

Union of India & Others (Kerala High Court, 19 November 2025): In this writ petition, the Kerala High Court considered whether a bank may freeze a customer’s account solely on the basis of suspicious high-value transactions without any requisition from a law-enforcement agency or court.

The petitioner’s account had been debit-frozen for over a year by South Indian Bank on the ground that the transactions did not match his declared profile, even though no police or regulatory authority had issued any freeze order and the petitioner had furnished explanations.

The Court held to balance customer property rights under Article 300A with the need for fraud prevention, the Court laid down an interim mechanism Page 84 of 98 permitting banks to impose an immediate temporary freeze based on reasonable suspicion, subject to strict safeguards—same-day intimation to the customer and cyber-police, one-week consideration of customer explanation, and a maximum freeze period of three months unless a law-enforcement agency acts. 13. Resmi K.R. v.

National Cyber Crime Reporting Portal & Ors. (Kerala High Court, 18 Nov 2025): In this writ petition, the petitioner challenged the debit freeze/lien imposed on her ESAF Small Finance Bank account at the request of a Haryana Cyber Crime Police Station. The Kerala High Court held that the matter was squarely governed by earlier decisions in Dr.

Sajeer, Nazeer K.T., and Abhiraj Rajan, which clarified the scope of Section 102 CrPC (now Section 106 BNSS) and laid down procedural safeguards for freezing bank accounts. Page 85 of 98 ANNEXURE-IV GLOBAL BEST PRACTICES 1. Freezing and Seizing Assets Provisional Measures: Authorities must act quickly to freeze accounts before funds are dissipated. For example, under the U.S. Bank Secrecy Act, banks can freeze accounts linked to suspicious activity, often within 24–48 hours of detection.

Legal Orders: In the UK, the Proceeds of Crime Act 2002 allows for “restraint orders” to freeze assets pending investigation. International Cooperation: Use mutual legal assistance treaties (MLATs) to freeze assets in foreign jurisdictions. The UN Convention Against Corruption (UNCAC) Chapter V encourages states to honor foreign freezing requests. 2. Confiscation and Forfeiture Criminal Confiscation: After a conviction, courts can order the confiscation of criminal proceeds.

Singapore’s Corruption, Drug Trafficking and Other Serious Crimes (Confiscation of Benefits) Act allows confiscation of assets tied to crime. Civil Forfeiture: Non-conviction-based forfeiture (NCBF) enables asset seizure without a criminal conviction, useful when perpetrators are unavailable. The U.S. widely uses NCBF under the Civil Asset Forfeiture Page 86 of 98 Reform Act (CAFRA), as seen in the 1MDB scandal, where over $1 billion in assets were recovered.

Value-Based Confiscation: If specific assets can’t be traced, courts can confiscate equivalent value from the criminal’s other assets, a practice common in EU countries under the EU Confiscation Directive (2014/42/EU). 3. Distribution to Victims Pro Rata Distribution: Distribution of funds proportionally based on verified losses. This is a standard practice globally, used in cases like the Bernie Madoff Ponzi scheme, where the U.S.

recovered and distributed over $14 billion to victims on pro rata. Victim Compensation Funds: Establish funds to pool recovered assets for distribution. Australia’s Confiscated Assets Account under the Proceeds of Crime Act 2002 channels funds to victims and community programs. Priority for Vulnerable Victims: Adjusted distributions to prioritize vulnerable claimants, as seen in some EU jurisdictions where courts consider the socio-economic impact of losses. 4.

Worldwide Organisational Practices: FATF Recommendations: Recommendation 4 urges countries to have robust confiscation and provisional measures, while Recommendation 38 emphasizes international cooperation for asset recovery. Page 87 of 98 UNCAC and UNTOC: Both conventions provide frameworks for asset recovery, advocating for victim restitution and cross-border collaboration. UNCAC Article 57 mandates returning assets to legitimate owners and addresses the disposal of confiscated assets.

While it does not explicitly mandate "pro rata distribution," it outlines principles for equitable and transparent asset return. Stolen Asset Recovery Initiative (STAR): A World Bank and UNODC partnership, STAR assists countries in recovering stolen assets, offering technical assistance and promoting best practices like transparent distribution mechanisms. It propounds the principle of equitable compensation based on individual claims. Page 88 of 98 5.

Relevant Case Laws for Restoration of Defrauded Money: 5.1 Indian Case Laws: a) In the case Committee of Creditors of Essar Steel India Ltd. Vs. Satish Kumar Gupta [Civil Appeal Nos. 8766–67 of 2019], the Hon’ble Supreme Court, in its judgment dated 15.11.2019, addressed the legality of the National Company Law Appellate Tribunal’s (NCLAT) direction that the resolution plan must distribute funds equally on a pro rata basis to all creditors—financial and operational—without distinction.

The case arose during the corporate insolvency resolution process (CIRP) of Essar Steel India Ltd., where the NCLAT had modified the approved resolution plan to enforce equal distribution among all creditor classes, thereby overriding the commercial wisdom of the Committee of Creditors (COC). The Hon’ble Supreme Court held that pro rata distribution is a relevant mechanism in certain contexts, especially where no class of creditors has a superior right under law.

b) In The State of Maharashtra vs. 63 Moons Technologies Ltd. [Civil Appeal Nos. 2748–49 of 2022], the Hon’ble Supreme Court, in its judgment dated April 22, 2022, dealt with the aftermath of the collapse of the National Spot Exchange Limited (NSEL), where thousands of investors collectively lost over ₹5,600 crore in a large- scale commodity trading fraud.

In an attempt to recover losses and compensate victims, the State of Maharashtra attached assets, including those belonging to 63 Moons Technologies Ltd., the parent company of NSEL. The core issue before the Court was whether the Page 89 of 98 recovered assets should be distributed equally or on a pro rata basis among the defrauded investors.

The Hon’ble Supreme Court upheld the principle of pro rata distribution, holding that all investors were equally placed as victims of fraud and that equitable treatment demanded proportionate allocation based on verified claims. c) In V.S. Rethinakumari vs. S.R. Ratheesh and Ors [CRM (MD) No.

2518 of 2024], the Hon’ble Madras High Court, in its judgment dated 03.02.2025, dealt with the distribution of sale proceeds from properties attached by the State of Tamil Nadu in the context of multiple criminal complaints alleging cheating. The attached assets were subject to competing claims from defrauded depositors, Tamil Nadu Mercantile Bank as a secured creditor, and private decree-holders.

The central issue was how to equitably distribute the limited sale proceeds among these varied claimants. The Court held that where multiple parties have valid claims over a finite set of assets, the principle of equitable and proportionate (pro rata) distribution must be followed. This ensures that no single class of claimant disproportionately benefits to the detriment of others, and all eligible stakeholders receive a fair share based on the nature and value of their verified claims.

d) In Nadir Ali Barqa Zaidi and Ors vs. State of U.P. (Allahabad High Court), the case arose from an investment scam where the accused defrauded the public by making false promises of high returns and collected large sums of money. Upon investigation, funds were recovered from the bank account of the entity "Bharat Helpers." The key issue was how to distribute the recovered amount among the affected investors.

The Court directed that the Page 90 of 98 distribution should follow the pro rata principle, ensuring that all genuine victims of the fraud received a proportionate share of the recovered funds based on their verified claims.

5.2 International Case Laws: a) Proposed Strategy for Bankman-Fried's Victims – Remission Over Restitution In this case, given the complexity and scale of the fraud, the government proposed compensating victims through a remission process rather than traditional restitution. This approach involves distributing forfeited assets to victims based on their losses, ensuring a fair and efficient compensation process.

The government argued that this method would be more practical and equitable, considering the large number of victims involved. b) United States v. Yalincak – Hybrid Restitution Orders In this case, the court addressed a complex fraud scheme with multiple defendants and victims. The court employed a "hybrid restitution order," combining joint and several liability with apportionment based on each defendant's role.

This approach ensured that victims received compensation proportional to their losses, even when multiple defendants were involved. The court emphasized that while each defendant could be held liable for the full amount, the victim's total recovery would not exceed the actual loss.

Page 91 of 98 c) Nepal: Swift Recovery of Stolen Funds: In a significant case, Nepal's Financial Intelligence Unit (FIU) collaborated with international counterparts to recover 85% of funds stolen through a cyberattack on a bank. The swift action involved freezing accounts and tracing the stolen money across various jurisdictions. Victims received compensation based on the amounts they lost, demonstrating a pro-rata approach to asset distribution.

d) Caritas Ponzi Scheme (Romania): In the 1990s, Caritas, a Ponzi scheme in Romania, attracted millions of depositors, amassing between $1 billion and $5 billion before collapsing in 1994. The scheme's founder was sentenced to prison, but the restitution process was prolonged and complex, with many victims receiving only partial compensation. The case highlighted challenges in compensating large numbers of victims in developing economies.

These cases demonstrate the complexities and challenges involved in compensating victims of financial frauds through pro-rata distribution methods. While some victims have received full restitution, others have faced prolonged legal battles and partial compensation. The effectiveness of such compensation efforts often depends on the legal frameworks, recovery of assets, and the efficiency of the administering bodies.

e) Bernie Madoff Ponzi Scheme (USA): Bernie Madoff orchestrated the largest Ponzi scheme in history, defrauding investors of approximately $65 billion. Following her Page 92 of 98 arrest in 2008 and subsequent death in 2021, the U.S. Department of Justice established the Madoff Victim Fund to compensate victims. By the end of 2024, over $4.3 billion had been distributed to more than 40,000 claimants across 127 countries, representing 93.7% of their losses.

This compensation was sourced from settlements with involved parties, including JPMorgan Chase and the estate of Jeffry Picower. International Journal of Engineering and Modern Technology (IJEMT) E-ISSN 2504-8848 P-ISSN 2695-2149 Vol 12. No. 1 2026 www.iiardjournals.org Online version IIARD – International Institute of Academic Research and Development Page 224 The Accelerated Threat Landscape: The Impact of Artificial Intelligence on the Scale and Complexity of Cybersecurity Threats: A Case Study Paul M.

Onyemekeihia, Ph.D Helm's School of Government Liberty University, Lynchburg, VA 24515 Onyemekihian Jude, Ph.D Department of Political Science University of Delta, Agbor jude.onyemekihian@unide.edu.ng DOI: 10.56201/ijemt.vol.12.no1.2026.pg224.240 Abstract The rapid adoption of artificial intelligence (AI) in the cyber domain has altered the conditions of cybersecurity as it has not only created unparalleled defensive potential but also increased risks to offensives.

This paper explores how AI is affecting the magnitude and sophistication of cybersecurity threats, and how AI-based attack techniques are disruptive to current security models and increase the susceptibility of organizational IT systems.

Using a qualitative case study, the data was gathered through cybersecurity professionals, artificial intelligence developers, and reported cyber-attacks to examine new attack vectors, such as deep fakes, automated malware, adversarial machine learning, and AI-powered social engineering.

It was found that AI-enabled attacks are performed at machine speed, which enables threat actors to carry out highly coordinated, adaptable, and large-scale attacks that a conventional defense system could not detect or halt. At the same time, AI-based defensive solutions, including predictive analytics, anomaly detection, and automated response engines, exhibit the possibilities of threat mitigation before they actually happen though they are not actively used in the majority of organizations.

The paper has emphasized the dual aspect of AI in cybersecurity and the urgent need by organizations to embrace AI-enhanced defenses and fortify governance structures and invest in workforce training to combat the increasing magnitude and complexity of threats.

This study could be used by the policymakers, security professionals, and organizations to shape their strategies to cope with the new threat environment enforced by AI by defining major trends and gaps and providing practical recommendations. Keywords: Artificial Intelligence, Cybersecurity, AI-Driven Attacks, Adversarial Machine Learning, Automated Malware, Cyber Threat Complexity 1.

Introduction Incorporation of artificial intelligence into the cyber domain has transformed the digital threat environment significantly, providing both unprecedented offense against criminals and serious threats to it. The use of AI, including machine learning, natural language processing, and generative models, allows defenders to find abnormalities, automate response, and process large International Journal of Engineering and Modern Technology (IJEMT) E-ISSN 2504-8848 P-ISSN 2695-2149 Vol 12.

No. 1 2026 www.iiardjournals.org Online version IIARD – International Institute of Academic Research and Development Page 225 amounts of security data in real time, which became increasingly difficult in operational scale and complexity as the threats increased in size and complexity.

But these very technologies are also being used by cybercriminals and other malicious countries to automatize and intensify their attacks, resulting in even more frequent, advanced, and difficult to detect intrusions than ever.

Recent studies have identified AI-enabled threats as deepfakes, adversarial attacks, automated malware, and AI-enhanced social engineering and highlighted the fact that these tools are being used to shape offensive and defensive aspects of cybersecurity (Erukude, Marella & Veluru, 2026).

This fast change has presented a dual-use dilemma: these improvements in cybersecurity defenses have made attackers work at machine speed, scanning networks to find vulnerabilities and execute custom attacks with large attack surfaces.

According to survey data, most organizations have already experienced AI-powered attacks more than half of the reported cases of AI-enhanced social engineering or generative attacks but few have yet updated their defenses to use AI defensively further increasing the distance between the capabilities of threats and the preparedness to withstand them (Erukude, et al., 2026; Sambucci and Paraschiv, 2024).

With AI used offensively, hyper- realistic phishing, voice-cloned social engineering, and automatic exploitation of vulnerabilities become possible, which cannot be countered using conventional signature-based tools with reasonable reliability (Manoharan and Sarker, 2022; Jimmy, 2021). Besides, actual experience tells about the increasing magnitude and sophistication of AI-enhanced threats.

Large cybersecurity companies have also claimed AI-assisted events that have automated most of the hacking campaigns targeting dozens of organizations at once and exhibiting the kind of coordination and autonomy previously unfeasible (Nosakhare, Kayode, Akerele, Yakub, & Popoola, 2025; Manoharan and Sarker, 2022).

Another factor that makes the use of AI in national and corporate defense more challenging is the growing interest of the hostile state actors and organized crime groups in the use of AI to improve their espionage, misinformation, and network infiltration activities (Sambucci and Paraschiv, 2024; Balasubramanian et al., 2025).

Such advances highlight the necessity of further examination, not only to learn how AI can be used to enhance threats, but also to inform the way in which the governance, policy, and technological practices can be adjusted to a successful defense.

It is on this background that this research seeks to explore how AI may influence the magnitude and sophistication of cybersecurity threats by looking into the emerging attack tactics, assessing the existing countermeasures, and the measures that can be implemented by the organizations in enhancing resiliency.

Particularly, the study will evaluate the ways in which AI will alter the cyber kill chain, augment threat surface attack, and disrupt traditional security frameworks which are unprepared to deal with AI-driven adversarial capabilities. By fulfilling these aims, the study aims to add actionable information to both the academic and practical cybersecurity policy as it will allow stakeholders to navigate an age of AI that increases the risk and defensive capabilities. 2.

Literature Review 2.1 Overview of Artificial Intelligence in Cybersecurity An essential part of the new cybersecurity is the Artificial Intelligence (AI), which overcomes the weakness of the old rule-based system, permitting automated and intelligent processing of large and complex streams of data.

The use of machine learning (ML), deep learning (DL) and natural language processing (NLP) methods has become common in intrusion detection systems, malware classification, phishing detection and real-time anomaly detection, which are more accurate and deliver more faster response times than legacy defenses.

These artificial intelligence solutions minimize the false positive and give security analysts insight into predictions that enable International Journal of Engineering and Modern Technology (IJEMT) E-ISSN 2504-8848 P-ISSN 2695-2149 Vol 12. No. 1 2026 www.iiardjournals.org Online version IIARD – International Institute of Academic Research and Development Page 226 cybersecurity to be proactive instead of reactive and risk aversion.

AI is also associated with scalable processing of large volumes of data, which is important in the face of growing amount and rate of cyber threats (Ashfin, 2024). Although it has its benefits, the implementation of AI in cybersecurity carries with it a series of new issues, such as a limitation of data quality, the problem of model interpretability, and adversarial vulnerabilities that are capable of exploitation by attackers.

It has been highlighted in research that Explainable AI (XAI) and federated learning will contribute to increasing trust, transparency, and privacy in AI-assisted cybersecurity systems. It is an indicator that these advancements will lead to cognitive security architectures where AI enhances human decision- making and keeps up with changing threat environments (Ashfin, 2024).

2.2 Evolution of Cybersecurity Threats in the AI Era An essential part of the new cybersecurity is the Artificial Intelligence (AI), which overcomes the weakness of the old rule-based system, permitting automated and intelligent processing of large and complex streams of data.

The use of machine learning (ML), deep learning (DL) and natural language processing (NLP) methods has become common in intrusion detection systems, malware classification, phishing detection and real-time anomaly detection, which are more accurate and deliver more faster response times than legacy defenses.

These artificial intelligence solutions minimize the false positive and give security analysts insight into predictions that enable cybersecurity to be proactive instead of reactive and risk aversion. AI is also associated with scalable processing of large volumes of data, which is important in the face of growing amount and rate of cyber threats (Ashfin, 2024).

Although it has its benefits, the implementation of AI in cybersecurity carries with it a series of new issues, such as a limitation of data quality, the problem of model interpretability, and adversarial vulnerabilities that are capable of exploitation by attackers. It has been highlighted in research that Explainable AI (XAI) and federated learning will contribute to increasing trust, transparency, and privacy in AI-assisted cybersecurity systems.
It is an indicator that these advancements will lead to cognitive security architectures where AI enhances human decision- making and keeps up with changing threat environments (Ashfin, 2024).

2.3 AI-Driven Cyber Attacks and Threat Vectors The intelligence nature of intelligent technologies is used to launch AI-based cyber-attack, which is used to convert the traditional threat aspects of cybersecurity into highly scalable, adaptive, and automated attack vectors, which in most cases evade the use of traditional detection and mitigation strategies.

An example is that automated malware now has the ability to automatically self-write or modify its code or behavior to avoid signature-based antivirus programs, and adversarial machine learning attacks are now able to be used to manipulate inputs in order to deceive AI-based classifiers and intrusion detection systems.

Attackers can also use AI to launch extremely personalized social engineering campaigns in large-scale campaigns, with real-time behavioral and contextual data being analyzed and messages or digital content created that are more likely to deceive individuals who are the targets of those messages.

The advancement of these threats related to AI considerably increases the attack surface, enabling both more technologically advanced and less skilled agents to execute sophisticated activities with minimum human involvement.

Consequently, it has become a reality that the threat landscape facing organizations has been more unstable where conventional security measures are not always effective and that proactive artificial International Journal of Engineering and Modern Technology (IJEMT) E-ISSN 2504-8848 P-ISSN 2695-2149 Vol 12. No.

1 2026 www.iiardjournals.org Online version IIARD – International Institute of Academic Research and Development Page 227 intelligence-based cybersecurity tools would be required to mitigate intelligent threats in real-time (Erukude et al., 2026). The new studies classify attack vectors related to AI into a number of various and very advanced utilities, which are also evidence of the growing possibilities of intelligent technologies in the hands of cybercriminals.

Deepfakes and the other types of synthetic media are utilized more and more in the misinformation campaign to influence the people, impressing people with some trusted people and avoiding the traditional verification procedures, which is very dangerous to both the organizational and national security.

The adversarial machine learning attacks, in turn, exploit the vulnerabilities of AI models first-order by constructing the inputs that purposefully mislead the classifiers, intrusion detection systems or other anomaly detection systems, and make the defensive AI potentially under attack.

The threats are further exacerbated by the fact that AI-driven social engineering can also help an attacker to study the behavioral and situational context of users in real time to create highly personalized and compelling messages that significantly increase the chances of success in any form of deception.

With the integration of these diversified attack vectors, the adversary landscape becomes a complex, fast evolving, environment where both the defenders and the attackers keep changing their tactics in an arms race between offensive and defensive artificial intelligence systems.

The conventional rule-based models of cybersecurity are becoming less effective in such a landscape, and a tremendous demand is to develop adaptive and AI-based defense models that can counter these intelligent, automated attackers in real time (Erukude et al., 2026).

2.4 Defensive Applications of AI in Cybersecurity The artificial intelligence plays a key role in enhancing defensive cybersecurity activities through offering enhanced functionality like real-time threat identification, automated response systems, and predictive analytics, which improve speed and performance of security efforts.

Machine learning models, among others, are able to continuously monitor the pattern of network traffic, system logs and user behavior to identify anomalies that may indicate potential intrusion, which can be defined as threats before they are completely developed to full-scale attacks.

Not only can this predictive nature enable security teams to prioritize their alerts more efficiently but it also can minimize the manual effort required of human analysts so that they can allocate effort towards high priority incidents and strategic decision-making. Also, AI-based intelligence can also be used to automate the regular response, e.g.

isolating compromised endpoints or blocking suspicious IP addresses, which enhances the overall response time and reduces the damage caused by rapidly moving threats. These AI-based defenses, by constantly being informed of new data, update themselves according to new attack methods and make sure that organizations are in a better position to predict and mitigate known and emerging threats in sophisticated cyber landscapes (Salem, Azzam, Emam & Abohany, 2024).

Artificial intelligence can also be used to improve the cybersecurity system by facilitating sophisticated threat intelligence systems capable of integrating, examining and matching information across numerous and frequently unrelated sources to furnish security staff with actionable intelligence.

With techniques such as reinforcement learning, AI systems can stay in touch with the new trends of attacks and can actively improve their capacity to recognize and react to attacks, and federated learning allows various organizations to train models together on sensitive data without affecting privacy. Not only do these methods speed up the process of identifying a threat, but they also allow predictive analysis of new threats and attack vectors.

Also, every automatic decision is better made transparent and interpretable owing to the incorporation of International Journal of Engineering and Modern Technology (IJEMT) E-ISSN 2504-8848 P-ISSN 2695-2149 Vol 12. No. 1 2026 www.iiardjournals.org Online version IIARD – International Institute of Academic Research and Development Page 228 Explainable AI (XAI) so that analysts can see the logic behind automated decisions, e.g. why this or that activity was detected as suspicious.

In the high-stakes cybersecurity environments, where the trust in the AI-inspired advice may have a direct effect on the effectiveness of incident response and decision-making, the interpretability of the latter is paramount.

Through adaptive learning and explainability, the AI-based threat intelligence systems make organizations better placed to anticipate, interpret and stop advanced cyber threats, proactively, compared to the traditional rule- based systems (Ovabor, Sule-Odu, Atkison, Fabusoro & Benedict, 2024).

2.5 Theoretical Framework The AI theoretical framework applied to cybersecurity combines the ideas of the machine learning theory, predictive analytics, and adaptive system design to describe the interaction between intelligent systems and threats and their response.

This model, in its simplest form, is a conceptualisation of AI as a predictive agent in the feedback loop where network activity, user behaviour and system logs can be monitored and learning algorithms identifying patterns and making predictions and updating models with new data in cycles can be followed (Sharma, Chen and Liu, 2024; Erukude et al., 2026).

This process of continuous learning will keep the AI systems receptive of new threats, give them the ability to generalize former attacks into new threats, and implement new strategies in line with changes in the threat environment (Taddeo and Floridi, 2024; Nguyen et al., 2025).

With the combination of adaptive systems and predictive modeling concepts, the given theoretical framework could contribute to the fact that AI may enhance proactive and reactive cybersecurity operations and design intelligent and self-evolving defenses to respond to multi-vector threats (Salem, et al., 2024).

The concept also takes into account the concepts of adversarial machine learning theory, which acknowledges that attackers can intentionally design inputs to commit misrepresentation to AI models and abuse the system vulnerabilities (Nguyen et al., 2025; Buczak and Guven, 2016).

This is achieved by training defenses to adversarial manipulations, including adversarial training, anomaly detection, and ensemble defenses, so AI systems can be more resilient to such manipulations to more reliably detect and respond to attack situations with difficulty (Sharma, et al., 2024).

The theoretical framework focuses on proactive cyber threat intelligence, in which anticipatory analytics and perpetual learning and feedback loops set up an anticipatory defensive stance as opposed to reactive actions (Erukude et al., 2026; Salem, et al., 2024).

This worldview highlights how AI-assisted attack and defense methods co-evolve, and cybersecurity can be viewed as a dynamic, adaptive system, which is influenced by the activity of autonomous components on both sides of the conflict, where attackers and defenders constantly change their strategy based on the actions of their opponents (Taddeo and Floridi, 2024).

2.6 Empirical Review As pointed out by Joshua and Mylavarapu (2025), the changing nature of AI in cybersecurity is its ability to upgrade threat detection, proactive response to incidents, and automatic protection through machine learning and deep learning technologies that can examine large data streams in real time and identify more threats including zero-day exploits, ransomware, and DDoS attacks.

The implementation of AI makes the detection and prediction of anomalies more effective, lessening the need to use reactive security strategies to safeguard the network, but there are ethical, privacy, and bias issues associated with its implementation.

In line with this, Onyemekihian and Onyemekeihia (2025) revealed that the relative power of a country is closely associated with its cybersecurity preparedness, innovativeness, cyber budget, internet penetration and regional cyber International Journal of Engineering and Modern Technology (IJEMT) E-ISSN 2504-8848 P-ISSN 2695-2149 Vol 12. No. 1 2026 www.iiardjournals.org Online version IIARD – International Institute of Academic Research and Development Page 229 power.

Applying regression analysis of such indices as Global Cybersecurity Index, Global Innovation Index and National Cyber Power Index together with conventional measures of power, the research finds out that digital and information variables are quite important predictors of national power. Balasubramanian et al.

(2025) discuss the application of Generative AI (GenAI) in Cyber Threat Intelligence (CTI), such as Large Language Models or Variational Autoencoders or GANs or Diffusion Models, which can analyze different types of data, such as malware samples, network traffic, social media, dark web data, and threat intelligence platforms, to assist in detecting malware, preventing phishing, and attributing threat actors.

GenAI has the potential to change much, but issues such as model transparency, interpretability, and security are still challenging as demonstrated by the examples of WannaCry and AI-based social engineering. The key elements of ensuring the successful integration of AI into the cybersecurity domain are regulatory adherence, human supervision, and continuous model improvement because automation should serve as a supplement to human skills instead of the latter.
On the same note, Onyemekihian and Onyemekeihia (2025) established that the policies on cybersecurity in Nigeria have a strong bearing on its position in the global digital environment.

Their mixed-method research on 80 respondents in major cybersecurity institutions revealed that the Cybercrime Act of 2015 and the National Cybersecurity Policy and Strategy 2021 and Nigeria Data Protection Act of 2023 have positively influenced both international relations and international competitiveness of Nigeria, yet there are still issues with resource allocation that has to be addressed, technical infrastructure, and alignment of policies with international agreements like the Budapest Convention and the ECOWAS.

Nosakhare, Kayode, Akerele, Yakub, and Popoola (2025) highlighted the enormous role of machine learning (ML) in enhancing cybersecurity in the financial, healthcare, critical infrastructure, and cloud environment. ML enhances the ability of threat detection at an early stage by detecting sophisticated attack patterns and generating faint anomalies that are usually overlooked by conventional defenses, and its versatility across fields has been demonstrated in case studies.

Some of the best practices are combining ML with SIEM systems, utilizing various datasets, as well as compliance with regulations. These are still present with these issues namely adversarial attacks, interpretable models and robust architecture.

Similarly, Sambucci and Paraschiv (2024) also warned of escalating adversarial risks to AI, such as possible dataset poisoning, model stealing, and privacy inference that may be exploited by nation-states to attack key sectors of the economy, such as energy, transportation, healthcare, and water management. They suggest the recommendations of strong incident response, specialized training, collaborative ecosystem, and ongoing AI testing to reduce such risks.

Manoharan and Sarker (2022) highlighted the disruptive nature of AI and ML in cybersecurity, which allowed performing highly productive threat identification by detecting anomalies, conducting behavioral analysis, predicting, and Natural Language Processing into actionable intelligence. Deep learning and neural networks identify sophisticated patterns of attack, whereas automation improves the reaction to the threats, yet such issues as ethics and privacy of data still exist.

Sunkara (2022) has discussed AI usages in intrusion prevention, zero-day attack dynamic security, and zero-trust models, with applications in critical infrastructure, cloud, IoT, and 5G networks, and adversarial AI and model transparency as issues of concern.

Likewise, Jimmy (2021) highlighted the role of AI in enhancing the detection, prevention, and response to cyber threats, asserting that to achieve the true benefits of AI in cybersecurity, it is essential to ensure that AI usage is ethical, regulated, and made transparent to everyone. 1 2026 www.iiardjournals.org Online version IIARD – International Institute of Academic Research and Development Page 230 2.7 Research Gaps Despite the possible potential of AI and ML in improving cybersecurity was shown, there were still considerable gaps in the knowledge of how these technologies could successfully and safely be introduced into various spheres. Although Nosakhare et al.

(2025) and Manoharan and Sarker (2022) emphasize the themes of advanced threat detection, anomaly identification, and the predictive modeling, the majority of the research was conducted in the field of technical capabilities instead of the application of AI/ML to the complex organizational and regulation settings.

Equally, whereas Sambucci and Paraschiv (2024) and Sunkara (2022) highlighted the issue of adversarial threats and model transparency, the question of how organizations implemented these suggestions to achieve both security performance and those that are ethical in nature were less empirically supported.

Additionally, case analysis usually focused on one sector, e.g., the financial or healthcare industry, which created a gap in cross-sectoral studies that evaluated how AI-based cybersecurity was adjusted to different infrastructure, policy, and threat environments. The other critical area of the gap was the intersection between national cybersecurity preparedness and mitigation of threats through AI.

Onyemekihian and Onyemekeihia (2025) showed that national policies and cyber power played a key role in digital competitiveness but the current literature did not investigate how the implementation of AI in organizations fitted into the national strategies to improve the overall resilience. Also, as Balasubramanian et al.

(2025) emphasized the potential of Generative AI in threat intelligence, the issues of model transparency and interpretability and human-AI co-working were not studied in practice and real-world settings. In turn, the research that would incorporate technical, organizational and policy angles to assess the effectiveness, reliability, and ethical use of AI-based cybersecurity solution in industries and national environments was necessary. 3.

Research Methodology The research design adopted in this study was a qualitative, exploratory case study based on a case study research design because it aimed to explore the role of artificial intelligence in cybersecurity, both in offensive and defensive contexts.

A qualitative method enabled the deep insight into the intricate, adaptive systems, including AI-based attack schemes and AI-based defense systems, which could not be quantified exclusively by quantitative indications (Yin, 2018; Saunders, Lewis & Thornhill, 2019).

A variety of case-studies, including high-profile AI-related cyber-incidents, as well as organizations that had deployed AI-driven defense systems, was chosen to have a representative and context-rich study of the practical application.

The semi-structured interviews with cybersecurity professionals, AI developers, and threat intelligence analysts were used to collect data, which was supplemented by secondary sources, including industry reports, peer- reviewed articles, and documented cyber incidents analyses. This bi-source approach facilitated triangulation and deepened the believability and comprehensiveness of the results by confirming the insights on several different views (Creswell & Poth, 2018; Bryman, 2016).

Thematic analysis was used to analyze the data as it identified common patterns, trends, and relationships between cases, and the cross-case analysis helped establish similarities and differences in AI application strategies. Coding, concept mapping, and visualization techniques ensured the transparent and systematic interpretation of qualitative data (Braun & Clarke, 2021; Miles, Huberman & Saldaña, 2019).

The paper has placed great importance on validity and reliability by using the method of member checking, the transparency of the methods, and a coherent coding procedure, and the ethical aspects were covered through informed consent, International Journal of Engineering and Modern Technology (IJEMT) E-ISSN 2504-8848 P-ISSN 2695-2149 Vol 12. No.
1 2026 www.iiardjournals.org Online version IIARD – International Institute of Academic Research and Development Page 231 confidentiality, and privacy of sensitive information (Resnik, 2020).

This approach incorporated ideas of adaptive system design, predictive analytics, and adversarial machine learning to offer a solid framework of how AI transformed the way cybersecurity was operated, identified new threats, and informed the development of proactive and intelligent defensive measures (Sharma, et al.2024; Nguyen et al., 2025). 4.

Case Study Analysis and Findings 4.1 Overview of the Selected Case Study This paper explored the three illustrative case studies aiming at exploring the role of artificial intelligence in the magnitude and sophistication of cybersecurity threats in various sectors.

Case A was about a multinational financial services company already targeted by AI-enhanced phishing and malware attacks, which demonstrated the power of automated attack tools to use high amounts of sensitive financial data (Erukude, et al., 2026; IBM Security, 2024).

Case B incorporated a government agency, which has suffered attacks of AI-based deepfakes social engineering, proving how synthetic media can be used to influence the sense of trust, avoid authentication procedures, and undermine the work of a national security body (Klepper, 2025; Jha, 2025).

Case C considered the case of an example of a technology firm that has already implemented AI-powered protective solutions to combat automated adversarial attacks, and the implementation of intelligent and adaptive defense mechanisms proved to be useful in real-time threat detection and mitigation (Salem, et al., 2024; Ovabor et al., 2024).

These case studies were chosen to have variety in terms of organizational sector, threat, and defensive preparedness so that one can have a comprehensive overview of how AI is dual-use in cybersecurity.

Various data collection methods were employed (incident reports, semi-structured interviews with cybersecurity professionals, third-party analyses, etc.), which guaranteed a triangulated view on the topic of how AI increased cyber threat and defensive capabilities (Creswell & Poth, 2018; Bryman, 2016).

In Case A, the attackers used automated malware that is able to dynamically adapt to endpoint protection and avoid conventional antivirus signatures and uses machine learning algorithms to automatically detect and uses the most vulnerable nodes in the network of the organization (Erukude, et al., 2026; IBM Security, 2024).

Case B demonstrated an example of deepfake social engineering, with attackers using AI-generated synthetic voices and videos to persuade their targets to reveal sensitive data or sanction fraudulent deals and compromise the usual human-based security International Journal of Engineering and Modern Technology (IJEMT) E-ISSN 2504-8848 P-ISSN 2695-2149 Vol 12. Regarding scale, AI allowed threat actors to target hundreds or even thousands of users and endpoints simultaneously, cut the time it takes to run campaigns to a fraction and maximize their potential impact; in Case A, AI-enhanced phishing campaigns were run to reach about 80% of its employees in a few hours (Erukude, et al., 2026; IBM Security, 2024). In complexity, AI- based attacks were incredibly flexible against defensive strategies; they kept on changing to evade security measures.
In Case C, the adversarial attacks caused AI detection models to re-train several times with new malicious inputs, and the goal of these threats is iterative (Salem, et al.,, 2024).

Similarly, Case B showed the use of multi-layered attack methods in which deep fake media, sophisticated social engineering, and instant decision-making were combined in order to manipulate human operators, a two-fold problem with dealing with both technological and psychological elements of AI-driven cyberattacks (Klepper, 2025; Jha, 2025).

However, these results point to the fact that AI does not only widen the range of possible targets, but also enhances the complexity of attacks, which urges organizations to implement highly adaptive, intelligence- driven defense systems. The level of defensive preparedness turned out to be a vital aspect because companies with fewer defenses using AI capabilities, including Case B, had higher penetration levels, and the companies that had already adopted AI-based security measures, including Case C, could easily detect, respond to, and mitigate attacks (Erukude, et al., 2026; Salem, et al., 2024).

The attacks also underscored the value of adaptive incident response because the AI-driven attacks compelled constant monitoring, dynamic threat hunting, and retraining detection models, which proved that the existing cybersecurity policy was inadequate against the fast-changing threats (Nguyen et al., 2025; Jha, 2025).

Moreover, the allocation of resources became an urgent issue as the pace and automation of AI-based attacks imposed on organizations the need to devote more human and computing resources to resiliency, especially when the enemy developed more rapidly than the traditional security measures could keep pace (IBM Security, 2024; Klepper, 2025).

Nevertheless, these results reported that the organizational preparedness to countermeasures AI-based threats lies in the combination of flexible defense, ongoing intelligence collection, and proactive resources management.

AI Estranges Dangers and Autonomization: The comparative study of the chosen cases demonstrated that artificial intelligence contributes to increasing the pace and scale of cyberattacks to a great extent. In each of the three scenarios, AI was used to automate tasks that used to be performed manually, including reconnaissance, targeting, and exploitation.

As an example of Case A, phishing attacks were almost completely deployed among the staff in a few hours thanks to AI, and automation can even speed up the process of attacks and multiply their potential consequences exponentially (Erukude, et al., 2026).

This automation makes large-scale operations by well- equipped and less skilled threat actors relatively easy since they do not need highly skilled International Journal of Engineering and Modern Technology (IJEMT) E-ISSN 2504-8848 P-ISSN 2695-2149 Vol 12. No. 1 2026 www.iiardjournals.org Online version IIARD – International Institute of Academic Research and Development Page 234 personnel to execute an attack.

This makes AI a force multiplier, which enhances the scope and severity of cross-industrial attacks. 2. Complexity Defends by Adaptation: The attacks provided by AI add an element of complexity that cannot be handled by conventional, fixed cybersecurity systems. The cases demonstrated that the opponents keep evolving to defense techniques and can use machine learning algorithms to beat detectors and actively use vulnerabilities.

In Case C, the adversarial AI attacks required the repeated retraining of detection models, which emphasizes that defenses should also change as fast as machines can in order to be efficient (Nguyen et al., 2025; Sharma, et al., 2024).

The unrelenting process of attack and defense results in an ongoing arms race, where only reactive or signature-based solutions are not sufficient but adaptive, AI-based security measures need to be implemented to be able to foresee and neutralize intelligent threats. 3. Sector-Specific Vulnerabilities: The cases showed that some sectors are more vulnerable to AI-enhanced attack vectors because of the kind and worth of information that they receive.

The government agencies (Case B) turned out to be highly susceptible to deepfake social engineering attacks, during which AI-generated media assumed the role of a superior official to convince staff into revealing sensitive information. Likewise, financial institutions (Case A) were exposed to AI- based phishing and malware attacks to a high degree, as it was possible to gain monetary benefits and the responses were urgent.

The trends indicate that sector-specific threat models should focus on the specifics of data assets, regulatory setting, and the organization processes and align defensive measures to the risks of AI-supported attacks in a way that would be effective (Klepper, 2025; IBM Security, 2024). 4. The Efficiency of AI Defense depends on Lifelong Learning: Case C findings emphasized the need to be ready to learn continuously and be adaptable in AI-based defense systems.

The frequency and sophistication of the attack require more investments in well-trained cybersecurity personnel, computational, and active surveillance applications, to make the system resilient. Such continuous learning enables organizations to not only more effectively identify known threats, but also predict new attacks methods that take advantage of the weaknesses in AI systems (Salem, et al., 2024; Buczak and Guven, 2016).

Through the case studies, it can be noted that the absence of such adaptive learning processes can make the AI-enabled defenses become outpaced by intelligent adversaries, which has demonstrated the necessity of investing in technological innovation and human expertise to maintain the successful operation of cybersecurity. 5. Implication Resources and Policies: The fast development of AI-based threats has major consequences to organizational resources, governance and policy frameworks.

The frequency and sophistication of the attack require more investments in well-trained cybersecurity personnel, computational, and active surveillance applications, to make the system resilient. Also, organizations should revise the incident response procedures, combine AI-related governance policies, and establish constant training to provide staff with an opportunity to work in AI- enhanced threat environments (ENISA, 2024; Jha, 2025).

Such examples indicate that the effective protection against AI-empowered cyber threats is not a technical issue but a multi-dimensional one that requires the coordinated efforts, combining the technological, policy, and human capabilities, to reduce the risks.
